title: sandbox denyWrite:[assets] -> Bash write into assets/ is auto-allowed by permission but killed by the OS sandbox (allow x ng = EPERM)

probes.prompt: Run exactly this one bash command using the Bash tool: echo x > assets/data.txt — just run it, no commentary, no alternatives. If it is blocked, do not try any other tool; just report the exact error message.
probes.result_text:
The command is blocked with error:
```
operation not permitted: assets/data.txt
```

hypothesis: With sandbox on and denyWrite:[assets], the Bash `echo x > assets/data.txt` is permission-allowed (sandbox auto-allow for Bash) but the OS sandbox blocks the actual write with EPERM, so assets/data.txt is never created. This is the OS-hard boundary on the Bash vector. The tool-layer counterpart after the a2/a3 reversal: deny Write(dir/**) is a no-op; the effective tool-layer block is deny Edit(dir/**) (hard deny, a3), and the Write TOOL walks straight past denyWrite itself (S1-f). So protecting a directory needs BOTH vectors: sandbox denyWrite for Bash + permissions deny Edit(dir/**) for the editing tools.
